snippets.title: CountVectorizer fit/transform split
snippets.code:
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

texts = ["free win now", "team meeting Monday", "free coffee Friday"]
labels = [1, 0, 0]

train_texts, test_texts, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    texts, labels, test_size=0.33, random_state=0
)

vec = CountVectorizer(stop_words="english")
X_train = vec.fit_transform(train_texts)
X_test = vec.transform(test_texts)

print(vec.get_feature_names_out())
print(X_train.toarray())
print(X_test.toarray())
snippets.explanation: `fit_transform` on train learns vocabulary and counts; `transform` on test reuses the same token-to-column map without seeing test strings during fit.

snippets.title: TfidfVectorizer with n-grams
snippets.code:
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=10000,
    ngram_range=(1, 2),
    min_df=2,
    stop_words="english",
)
X = tfidf.fit_transform(corpus)

# Inspect top-weighted features for document 0
row = X[0].toarray().ravel()
top_idx = row.argsort()[-5:][::-1]
for i in top_idx:
    print(tfidf.get_feature_names_out()[i], row[i])
snippets.explanation: `ngram_range=(1,2)` adds bigrams like "not good"; `min_df=2` drops tokens appearing in fewer than two documents—common text extraction hygiene.

snippets.title: Pipeline with vectorizer + classifier
snippets.code:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("vec", CountVectorizer(max_features=5000, stop_words="english")),
    ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)),
])

scores = cross_val_score(pipe, texts, labels, cv=3)
print(scores.mean())

pipe.fit(texts, labels)
pipe.predict(["win free prize"])
snippets.explanation: Cross-validation refits the vectorizer inside each fold on training folds only— the correct pattern for tuning extraction + model together.

snippets.title: HashingVectorizer for large vocabularies
snippets.code:
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import HashingVectorizer

hasher = HashingVectorizer(
    n_features=2**16,
    alternate_sign=False,
    norm="l2",
)

# No fit needed — stateless hashing
X_train = hasher.transform(train_texts)
X_test = hasher.transform(test_texts)
snippets.explanation: HashingVectorizer avoids storing a vocabulary; use when unique token counts explode. Pair with linear models that tolerate approximate features.

snippets.title: ColumnTransformer: text + numeric columns
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "text": ["refund please", "love this product", "broken screen"],
    "tenure_days": [30, 400, 12],
})
y = [1, 0, 1]

preprocess = ColumnTransformer([
    ("text", TfidfVectorizer(max_features=1000), "text"),
    ("num", StandardScaler(), ["tenure_days"]),
])

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("prep", preprocess),
    ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)),
])
pipe.fit(df, y)
snippets.explanation: Different extractors run on different columns then concatenate—still one Pipeline with a single fit/transform contract.
